title: Preface | Tools Keep Getting Stronger. Why Do Many People Become More Chaotic?
summary: The tool explosion promises efficiency, yet many people become more scattered because tools are not connected to a clear system.
paragraphs: Preface | Tools Keep Getting Stronger. Why Do Many People Become More Chaotic?
The tool explosion promises efficiency, yet many people become more scattered because tools are not connected to a clear system.
Core idea: Tools create value only when they reduce cognitive load and serve a coherent workflow.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: List every tool you used yesterday and mark whether each one reduced or increased friction.
This English preview is a concise adaptation for the bilingual site. The structure is ready for a fuller English manuscript without changing the page code.

section: Part One | Build the Right View of Tools: Tools Are Leverage, Not Answers
title: Chapter 1 | Why Many People Become Less Efficient the More Tools They Use
summary: More tools can increase switching costs, fragmented storage, duplicated work, and false productivity.
paragraphs: Chapter 1 | Why Many People Become Less Efficient the More Tools They Use
More tools can increase switching costs, fragmented storage, duplicated work, and false productivity.
Core idea: Tool overload often hides a missing workflow, unclear criteria, or weak habits.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Choose one tool you rarely use and decide whether to integrate it, replace it, or delete it.

section: Part One | Build the Right View of Tools: Tools Are Leverage, Not Answers
title: Chapter 2 | What Tools Really Are: Externalization, Standardization, Amplification
summary: Tools move thought outside the brain, standardize repeated work, and amplify ability through storage, automation, and collaboration.
paragraphs: Chapter 2 | What Tools Really Are: Externalization, Standardization, Amplification
Tools move thought outside the brain, standardize repeated work, and amplify ability through storage, automation, and collaboration.
Core idea: A tool should make important work easier to see, repeat, hand off, or scale.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: For one workflow, identify what should be externalized, standardized, and amplified.

section: Part One | Build the Right View of Tools: Tools Are Leverage, Not Answers
title: Chapter 3 | More Tools Are Not Better; Better-Fitting Tools Are Better
summary: A small set of fitting tools is better than a large stack that looks advanced but increases friction.
paragraphs: Chapter 3 | More Tools Are Not Better; Better-Fitting Tools Are Better
A small set of fitting tools is better than a large stack that looks advanced but increases friction.
Core idea: Fit means the tool matches your task type, thinking style, collaboration needs, and maintenance capacity.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Name your core tool stack and write the job each tool is responsible for.

section: Part One | Build the Right View of Tools: Tools Are Leverage, Not Answers
title: Chapter 4 | How Tools, Methods, and Models Work Together
summary: Tools should support methods, and methods should serve models. When the chain is broken, tools become decoration or distraction.
paragraphs: Chapter 4 | How Tools, Methods, and Models Work Together
Tools should support methods, and methods should serve models. When the chain is broken, tools become decoration or distraction.
Core idea: The strongest workflow connects why, how, and with what.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Take one tool and write the model and method that should govern its use.

section: Part Two | Information System: Build Order in an Age of Overload
title: Chapter 5 | More Information Is Not Better; Processable Information Matters
summary: Information has value only when it can be selected, understood, connected, retrieved, and used.
paragraphs: Chapter 5 | More Information Is Not Better; Processable Information Matters
Information has value only when it can be selected, understood, connected, retrieved, and used.
Core idea: An information system should improve signal quality rather than expand noise volume.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Audit one information source and decide whether it is signal, reference, entertainment, or noise.

section: Part Two | Information System: Build Order in an Age of Overload
title: Chapter 6 | Collection System: Catch External Information First, but Do Not Build a Junkyard
summary: A collection system should capture useful inputs quickly while preventing indiscriminate hoarding.
paragraphs: Chapter 6 | Collection System: Catch External Information First, but Do Not Build a Junkyard
A collection system should capture useful inputs quickly while preventing indiscriminate hoarding.
Core idea: Capture is only the first step; without filtering and review, it becomes another pile.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Design one inbox rule: what enters, what is ignored, when it is reviewed, and where it goes next.

section: Part Two | Information System: Build Order in an Age of Overload
title: Chapter 7 | Organization System: Move from Piles to Categories, Links, and Retrieval
summary: Collected information becomes useful when it is grouped, connected, summarized, and prepared for future retrieval.
paragraphs: Chapter 7 | Organization System: Move from Piles to Categories, Links, and Retrieval
Collected information becomes useful when it is grouped, connected, summarized, and prepared for future retrieval.
Core idea: Organization is not neatness for its own sake; it is preparation for later thinking and action.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Take ten saved notes and organize them into categories, links, and one short summary.

section: Part Two | Information System: Build Order in an Age of Overload
title: Chapter 8 | Retrieval System: You Only Own What You Can Find
summary: A knowledge asset is not truly yours if it cannot be found when needed. Retrieval completes the information loop.
paragraphs: Chapter 8 | Retrieval System: You Only Own What You Can Find
A knowledge asset is not truly yours if it cannot be found when needed. Retrieval completes the information loop.
Core idea: Good retrieval depends on naming, structure, search habits, tags, links, and review rituals.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Search for a note you needed recently and improve the title, tags, or folder that made it hard to find.

section: Part Two | Information System: Build Order in an Age of Overload
title: Chapter 9 | Information Restraint: Truly Efficient People Do Not Read Everything
summary: Efficiency requires restraint. The best information system includes refusal, filtering, and deliberate attention.
paragraphs: Chapter 9 | Information Restraint: Truly Efficient People Do Not Read Everything
Efficiency requires restraint. The best information system includes refusal, filtering, and deliberate attention.
Core idea: Information restraint protects attention for judgment, creation, and deep work.
How to read it: treat the chapter as a working frame. Identify the situation it describes, the mistake it warns against, and the standard it asks you to build into your own system.
Static practice: Choose one source to pause for a week and record what attention it gives back.
